examples.q: What is the hydrostatic pressure at a depth of 5 m in fresh water? (ρ = 1 000 kg/m³, g = 10 m/s²)
examples.solution:
Hydrostatic pressure is the pressure exerted by
a fluid at rest due to the weight of fluid above.

Formula:
    P = ρ × g × h

where  P = hydrostatic pressure  (Pa)
       ρ = density of fluid      (kg/m³)
       g = gravitational field   (10 m/s² on Earth)
       h = depth below surface   (m)

Step 1 — Identify the values:
    ρ = 1 000 kg/m³
    g = 10 m/s²
    h = 5 m

Step 2 — Substitute and solve:
    P = 1 000 × 10 × 5
    P = 50 000 Pa

Answer:  P = 50 000 Pa  (50 kPa)

Note: Pressure increases linearly with depth.

examples.q: A diver descends to 12 m in seawater (ρ = 1 025 kg/m³, g = 10 m/s²). The atmospheric pressure at the surface is 101 325 Pa. What is the total (absolute) pressure at that depth?
examples.solution:
Total (absolute) pressure at depth = atmospheric
pressure at the surface PLUS hydrostatic pressure:

    P_total = P_atm + ρgh

Step 1 — Calculate hydrostatic pressure:
    P_hydro = ρ × g × h
    P_hydro = 1 025 × 10 × 12
    P_hydro = 123 000 Pa

Step 2 — Add atmospheric pressure:
    P_total = 101 325 + 123 000
    P_total = 224 325 Pa

Answer:  P_total ≈ 224 325 Pa (≈ 224.3 kPa)

Note: Gauge pressure = P_hydro only (ignores P_atm).
Absolute pressure always includes P_atm.

examples.q: Two connected containers are filled with water (ρ = 1 000 kg/m³). Container A has a depth of 3 m and Container B has a depth of 3 m. How does the pressure at the bottom of A compare to B?
examples.solution:
This demonstrates that hydrostatic pressure depends
ONLY on depth and fluid density — NOT on the shape
or volume of the container.

Formula:  P = ρgh

Container A:
    P_A = 1 000 × 10 × 3 = 30 000 Pa

Container B:
    P_B = 1 000 × 10 × 3 = 30 000 Pa

Answer:  P_A = P_B = 30 000 Pa

This is called the Hydrostatic Paradox:
  The pressure at a given depth is the same
  regardless of the shape, width, or total volume
  of the container — only depth matters.
opts: P_A = P_B — pressure depends only on depth, not container shape
P_A > P_B — wider containers have higher pressure
P_B > P_A — narrower containers have higher pressure
Cannot be determined without knowing container shape

questions.q: What is the hydrostatic pressure at a depth of 8 m in fresh water? (ρ = 1 000 kg/m³, g = 10 m/s²)
What factors determine the hydrostatic pressure at a point in a fluid?
questions.given:
Formula:  P = ρ × g × h
Examine what variables appear in the equation.
opts: Fluid density, gravitational field strength, and depth
Container shape, fluid mass, and depth
questions.sol: P = ρgh — only fluid density (ρ), gravitational field strength (g), and depth (h) determine hydrostatic pressure.

questions.q: The hydrostatic pressure at a certain depth in water is 40 000 Pa. (ρ = 1 000 kg/m³, g = 10 m/s²). What is the depth?
questions.given:
Rearrange:  P = ρ × g × h
→  h = P / (ρ × g)

questions.q: Seawater has a density of 1 025 kg/m³. What is the hydrostatic pressure at a depth of 20 m? (g = 10 m/s²)
In which direction does hydrostatic pressure act at a point inside a fluid?
questions.given:
Think about the nature of pressure in a fluid at rest.
A fluid cannot exert shear forces.
questions.sol: Hydrostatic pressure at any point acts equally in all directions. Fluids at rest transmit pressure uniformly.

questions.q: A large wide tank and a narrow tall cylinder are both filled with water to the same depth of 6 m. How does the pressure at the bottom compare?
questions.given:
Formula:  P = ρgh
Pressure depends on depth, not container shape or volume.
opts: Equal — pressure depends only on depth
Higher in the wide tank — more water above
Higher in the narrow cylinder — water is more compressed
Cannot be determined without knowing the volumes
questions.sol: P = ρgh. Since depth and density are the same, pressure is equal. This is the Hydrostatic Paradox.

questions.q: What is the gauge pressure at a depth of 10 m in fresh water? (ρ = 1 000 kg/m³, g = 10 m/s²)
questions.given:
Gauge pressure = hydrostatic pressure only (excludes atmospheric pressure).
Formula:  P_gauge = ρgh
questions.sol: P_gauge = ρgh = 1 000 × 10 × 10 = 100 000 Pa. Gauge pressure ignores atmospheric pressure.

questions.q: A submarine is at a depth where the hydrostatic pressure is 500 000 Pa. (ρ = 1 025 kg/m³, g = 10 m/s²). What is the approximate depth?
questions.given:
Rearrange:  P = ρ × g × h
→  h = P / (ρ × g)

questions.q: Why does a dam need to be thicker at the base than at the top?
questions.given:
Consider how hydrostatic pressure varies with depth:
P = ρgh — pressure increases with depth.
opts: Pressure increases with depth, so the base experiences the greatest force
The base supports the weight of the dam itself
Water flows faster at the base, creating more force
The base needs more material to prevent flooding
questions.sol: P = ρgh — hydrostatic pressure increases with depth. The base of the dam faces the highest pressure and needs the most structural strength.

questions.q: Fresh water (ρ = 1 000 kg/m³) and oil (ρ = 800 kg/m³) are each at a depth of 5 m. Which has greater hydrostatic pressure? (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.given:
Formula:  P = ρ × g × h
Calculate and compare pressure for each fluid.
opts: Water — P = 50 000 Pa vs oil P = 40 000 Pa
Oil — lower density means less resistance
questions.sol: P_water = 1 000 × 10 × 5 = 50 000 Pa. P_oil = 800 × 10 × 5 = 40 000 Pa. Water has higher pressure due to greater density.
